title: Contract Management
description: Quiz covering contract management, supplier relationships, contract administration, changes, risks and practical project management scenarios.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of contract management?
options: To ensure both parties fulfill their contractual obligations throughout the project
To eliminate the need for project planning
To reduce the number of project stakeholders
To create the project budget
questions.explanation: Contract management ensures that both parties meet the obligations agreed upon in the contract.

questions.question: When does contract management normally begin?
options: After the contract has been signed
Before procurement planning
After the project has finished
During project initiation
questions.explanation: Contract management begins once the contract has been awarded and signed.

questions.question: What is a contract?
options: A legally binding agreement between two or more parties
A project schedule
A stakeholder register
An informal verbal discussion
questions.explanation: A contract is a legally enforceable agreement that defines rights and obligations.

questions.question: Which of the following is typically included in a contract?
options: Deliverables, payment terms and responsibilities
Employees' vacation schedules
Company marketing strategy
The project manager's personal objectives
questions.explanation: Contracts normally define scope, deliverables, responsibilities, schedule, payment terms and quality requirements.

questions.question: Which project activity is most closely related to contract management?
options: Monitoring supplier performance
Writing the project charter
Creating the Work Breakdown Structure
Conducting stakeholder identification
questions.explanation: One of the main responsibilities of contract management is monitoring supplier performance.

questions.question: Why are written contracts important in projects?
options: They clearly define each party's responsibilities and reduce misunderstandings
They eliminate every project risk
They guarantee the project will finish on time
They remove the need for communication
questions.explanation: Clear contracts reduce ambiguity by defining expectations, responsibilities and procedures.

questions.question: A supplier has completed part of the contracted work. Before approving payment, the project manager should first:
options: Verify that the agreed deliverables have been completed
Pay immediately to maintain a good relationship
Ask another supplier for advice
Update the stakeholder register
questions.explanation: Payments should normally be approved only after verifying that contractual deliverables have been completed.

questions.question: Which document normally defines how contract changes should be handled?
options: The contract itself
The organizational chart
The meeting agenda
The project calendar
questions.explanation: Most contracts include formal procedures for requesting, evaluating and approving changes.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of contract management?
options: Reviewing whether a supplier has met agreed quality requirements
Interviewing new employees
Preparing the company budget
Creating a marketing campaign
questions.explanation: Monitoring supplier performance against contractual quality requirements is a key contract management activity.

questions.question: A supplier informs the project manager that delivery will be delayed by three weeks. What should the project manager do first?
options: Review the contract, assess the project impact and discuss possible recovery actions
Terminate the contract immediately
Ignore the delay because suppliers are responsible
Approve additional payments automatically
questions.explanation: The first step is to review the contractual obligations, understand the impact and work with the supplier to identify possible solutions.

questions.question: A client requests additional features after the contract has been signed. What should the project manager do first?
options: Follow the contract's formal change management procedure
Approve the work immediately
Reject the request without discussion
Ask the supplier to absorb the additional work for free
questions.explanation: Contract changes should be handled through the agreed change management process to evaluate impacts on cost, schedule and scope.

questions.question: Why should contract changes be documented?
options: To ensure both parties agree on the revised obligations
To make the contract longer
To reduce communication with suppliers
To avoid updating the project schedule
questions.explanation: Documenting changes prevents misunderstandings and provides a clear record of what has been approved.

questions.question: Which situation represents the greatest contract management risk?
options: A supplier repeatedly misses agreed delivery dates
A supplier submits progress reports on time
A project manager documents meeting minutes
A client approves completed work
questions.explanation: Repeated delivery delays can directly affect project schedule, cost and stakeholder satisfaction.

questions.question: A supplier has delivered equipment that does not meet the agreed technical specifications. What should the project manager do?
options: Compare the deliverables against the contract requirements before accepting them
Accept the equipment to avoid conflict
Pay the supplier immediately
Ignore the quality issue because delivery was on time
questions.explanation: Deliverables should always be verified against the contract before acceptance and payment.

questions.question: Why are regular meetings with suppliers valuable during contract execution?
options: They help identify and resolve issues before they become major disputes
They eliminate the need for contracts
They reduce project scope
They replace progress reports
questions.explanation: Regular communication promotes collaboration and allows problems to be addressed early.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the relationship between contract management and supplier relationships?
options: Successful contract management combines contractual control with effective collaboration
Contract management is only about enforcing penalties
Good supplier relationships eliminate the need for contracts
Supplier relationships are unrelated to project success
questions.explanation: Professional contract management balances contractual enforcement with productive working relationships.

questions.question: A supplier requests additional payment because the client changed the project requirements. What should happen next?
options: The request should be evaluated through the agreed contract change procedure
The payment should be approved immediately
The supplier should be ignored
The contract should automatically be terminated
questions.explanation: Changes affecting scope, cost or schedule should be formally evaluated before approval.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of documenting project communication with suppliers?
options: It provides evidence of decisions and reduces future misunderstandings
It replaces the signed contract
It guarantees the supplier will never make mistakes
It eliminates the need for project meetings
questions.explanation: Written records improve accountability and help resolve disagreements if they arise.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of effective contract management?
options: Monitoring milestones, verifying deliverables and addressing issues as they arise
Only reading the contract after a dispute occurs
Approving all invoices without review
Avoiding communication with suppliers to prevent conflict
questions.explanation: Contract management is an ongoing process that includes monitoring, verification and proactive issue resolution.

questions.question: Why is proactive contract management generally more effective than relying on penalties after problems occur?
options: Because preventing problems is usually less costly and less disruptive than resolving disputes later
Because penalties are never allowed in contracts
Because suppliers never make mistakes
Because contracts automatically solve all disagreements
questions.explanation: Successful project managers focus on preventing problems through communication and monitoring rather than resolving expensive disputes after they have escalated.

questions.question: A project manager notices that a supplier has missed several intermediate milestones but is still expected to finish on time. What is the best course of action?
options: Meet with the supplier to understand the delays and agree on corrective actions before the final deadline is affected
Ignore the missed milestones until the final delivery date
Immediately terminate the contract
Automatically approve additional payments
questions.explanation: Intermediate milestones are early warning indicators. Addressing issues early reduces the likelihood of major schedule delays.

questions.question: Why should project managers regularly compare supplier performance against the contract?
options: To verify that work is being completed according to the agreed requirements
To renegotiate the contract every week
To reduce communication with stakeholders
To eliminate project risks
questions.explanation: Regular performance monitoring helps identify deviations before they become serious project problems.

questions.question: Which situation is most likely to result in a contractual dispute?
options: The contract contains vague or unclear deliverable descriptions
Both parties document all approved changes
The supplier provides regular progress updates
The client approves completed deliverables after inspection
questions.explanation: Poorly defined requirements often lead to different interpretations of what should be delivered.

questions.question: A supplier proposes a design improvement that would reduce project costs without affecting quality. What should the project manager do?
options: Evaluate the proposal using the contract's change management process before making a decision
Reject the proposal because contracts cannot be changed
Accept the proposal immediately without documentation
Ignore the supplier's suggestion
questions.explanation: Even beneficial changes should be evaluated and formally approved through the agreed contract procedures.

questions.question: What is one of the greatest benefits of maintaining a strong relationship with suppliers?
options: Problems are more likely to be solved collaboratively before they escalate
Contracts are no longer necessary
The supplier automatically lowers all prices
The project manager no longer needs to monitor performance
questions.explanation: Strong professional relationships encourage openness, trust and early problem-solving.

questions.question: A client verbally requests additional work during a meeting. What should the project manager do?
options: Document the request and follow the formal contract change procedure before work begins
Start the work immediately
Ignore the client's request
Ask the supplier to complete the work without compensation
questions.explanation: Verbal requests should always be documented and processed formally to avoid future disagreements.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the role of a project manager in contract management?
options: To balance contractual compliance, project objectives and productive supplier relationships
To enforce penalties whenever possible
To avoid communicating with suppliers
To delegate all contract responsibilities to the legal department
questions.explanation: Successful contract management combines contractual control with collaboration and effective communication.

questions.question: A project is progressing well, but the supplier has stopped submitting the monthly reports required by the contract. Why is this a concern?
options: Reporting requirements are contractual obligations that support project monitoring and transparency
Reports are optional once work has started
Only financial reports matter
Monthly reports are only needed when the project is behind schedule
questions.explanation: Contractual reporting requirements help both parties monitor progress and identify issues early.

questions.question: Which approach is most likely to prevent expensive legal disputes during a project?
options: Regular communication, documented decisions, early issue resolution and adherence to the contract
Avoiding communication until the project is complete
Only discussing problems after they have escalated
Ignoring minor disagreements between the parties
questions.explanation: Most contract disputes can be avoided through proactive management, clear communication and proper documentation.

questions.question: What is the ultimate objective of effective contract management?
options: To ensure that contractual obligations are fulfilled while supporting successful project delivery through collaboration, monitoring and controlled changes
To maximize the number of contractual penalties
To minimize communication between the parties
To prevent any future contracts with the supplier
questions.explanation: Contract management is about ensuring successful project delivery by balancing contractual compliance, supplier performance, communication and change management.
